questions.stem: This scheme depicts the biotransformation of xenobiotics through two sequential stages. What is the overall functional purpose of processing a compound through these reactions?

choices.label: A
choices.text: To convert every drug into its pharmacologically active form

choices.label: B
choices.text: To increase polarity and water solubility so the compound can be excreted

choices.label: C
choices.text: To decrease renal clearance of the compound

choices.label: D
choices.text: To generate nonpolar, volatile metabolites exhaled by the lungs

choices.label: E
choices.text: To increase lipid solubility for storage in adipose tissue

explanation.summary: Phase I reactions (often CYP450-mediated oxidation/reduction/hydrolysis) add or expose functional groups, and Phase II reactions conjugate polar groups. The net effect is greater water solubility, facilitating renal or biliary excretion.
explanation.whyCorrect: Phase I reactions (often CYP450-mediated oxidation/reduction/hydrolysis) add or expose functional groups, and Phase II reactions conjugate polar groups. The net effect is greater water solubility, facilitating renal or biliary excretion.

questions.stem: This diagram of acetaminophen metabolism shows a toxic intermediate (NAPQI) formed via CYP2E1. Why does chronic alcohol use increase the risk of acetaminophen hepatotoxicity?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Alcohol blocks intestinal absorption of N-acetylcysteine

choices.label: B
choices.text: Alcohol directly binds acetaminophen in the renal tubules

choices.label: C
choices.text: Alcohol depletes the sulfate needed by sulfotransferase

choices.label: D
choices.text: Alcohol competitively inhibits glucuronidation of acetaminophen

choices.label: E
choices.text: Alcohol induces CYP2E1, increasing production of the toxic intermediate NAPQI

explanation.summary: Chronic ethanol induces CYP2E1, shunting more acetaminophen toward NAPQI. When glutathione (GSH) is depleted, NAPQI binds cellular proteins and causes centrilobular hepatic necrosis. N-acetylcysteine treats this by replenishing GSH.
explanation.whyCorrect: Chronic ethanol induces CYP2E1, shunting more acetaminophen toward NAPQI. When glutathione (GSH) is depleted, NAPQI binds cellular proteins and causes centrilobular hepatic necrosis. N-acetylcysteine treats this by replenishing GSH.

questions.stem: The red drug in this hepatic pathway blocks the enzyme that catalyzes the committed, rate-limiting step of cholesterol synthesis. Why does inhibiting this specific step effectively lower cholesterol production?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It regenerates NADPH needed elsewhere

choices.label: B
choices.text: It is the rate-limiting step, so blocking it throttles the entire downstream pathway

choices.label: C
choices.text: It is the only irreversible step after squalene

choices.label: D
choices.text: It directly degrades circulating LDL

choices.label: E
choices.text: It is the final step that directly forms cholesterol

explanation.summary: HMG-CoA reductase catalyzes the rate-limiting conversion of HMG-CoA to mevalonate. Statins competitively inhibit this enzyme; because it controls flux through the whole pathway, blocking it markedly reduces cholesterol synthesis and upregulates hepatic LDL receptors.
explanation.whyCorrect: HMG-CoA reductase catalyzes the rate-limiting conversion of HMG-CoA to mevalonate. Statins competitively inhibit this enzyme; because it controls flux through the whole pathway, blocking it markedly reduces cholesterol synthesis and upregulates hepatic LDL receptors.

questions.stem: This figure shows aspirin transferring its acetyl group onto a serine side chain of the COX-1 and COX-2 polypeptide. What is the functional consequence of this covalent modification for the enzyme?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Reversible competitive inhibition of the enzyme

choices.label: B
choices.text: Irreversible inhibition of cyclooxygenase activity

choices.label: C
choices.text: Conversion of the enzyme into a lipoxygenase

choices.label: D
choices.text: Increased prostaglandin synthesis

choices.label: E
choices.text: Allosteric activation of the active site

explanation.summary: Aspirin acetylates a serine residue in the COX active site, covalently and irreversibly blocking the channel to arachidonic acid. Because the block is permanent, cells (notably platelets) must synthesize new enzyme to recover activity.
explanation.whyCorrect: Aspirin acetylates a serine residue in the COX active site, covalently and irreversibly blocking the channel to arachidonic acid. Because the block is permanent, cells (notably platelets) must synthesize new enzyme to recover activity.

questions.stem: In this eicosanoid pathway, glucocorticoids act at an earlier step than NSAIDs. Why does this earlier point of action give glucocorticoids a broader anti-inflammatory effect?

choices.label: A
choices.text: They block leukotriene receptors on immune cells

choices.label: B
choices.text: They block phospholipase, preventing formation of both prostaglandins and leukotrienes

choices.label: C
choices.text: They inhibit only cyclooxygenase, like NSAIDs

choices.label: D
choices.text: They accelerate breakdown of arachidonic acid

choices.label: E
choices.text: They directly neutralize prostaglandins already formed

explanation.summary: Glucocorticoids inhibit phospholipase, stopping release of arachidonic acid upstream of both the cyclooxygenase (prostaglandin) and lipoxygenase (leukotriene) branches. NSAIDs act downstream at cyclooxygenase only, sparing leukotriene synthesis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Glucocorticoids inhibit phospholipase, stopping release of arachidonic acid upstream of both the cyclooxygenase (prostaglandin) and lipoxygenase (leukotriene) branches. NSAIDs act downstream at cyclooxygenase only, sparing leukotriene synthesis.

questions.stem: In this pancreatic beta-cell, sulfonylureas act at the transporter marked in step 3. What is their immediate mechanistic effect?

choices.label: A
choices.text: They stimulate ATP synthesis in the mitochondria

choices.label: B
choices.text: They increase GLUT2 glucose uptake

choices.label: C
choices.text: They open voltage-gated Ca2+ channels directly

choices.label: D
choices.text: They trigger insulin exocytosis independent of Ca2+

choices.label: E
choices.text: They block the ATP-sensitive K+ channel, causing membrane depolarization

explanation.summary: Sulfonylureas close the ATP-sensitive K+ channel, mimicking the effect of high intracellular ATP. The resulting depolarization opens voltage-gated Ca2+ channels, and the calcium influx triggers insulin granule fusion and exocytosis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Sulfonylureas close the ATP-sensitive K+ channel, mimicking the effect of high intracellular ATP. The resulting depolarization opens voltage-gated Ca2+ channels, and the calcium influx triggers insulin granule fusion and exocytosis.

questions.stem: In this cycle, the drug shown blocks epoxide reductase, preventing regeneration of the reduced vitamin needed by the carboxylase. What is the direct consequence for the coagulation proteins?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Accelerated hepatic synthesis of clotting factors

choices.label: B
choices.text: Direct proteolytic cleavage of fibrinogen

choices.label: C
choices.text: Increased calcium binding and hyperactive factors

choices.label: D
choices.text: Enhanced membrane anchoring of active factors

choices.label: E
choices.text: Impaired gamma-carboxylation of glutamate residues, yielding inactive factors

explanation.summary: Warfarin inhibits vitamin K epoxide reductase, so reduced vitamin K cannot be regenerated. Without it, gamma-glutamyl carboxylase cannot carboxylate Glu residues to Gla, leaving factors II, VII, IX, X unable to bind Ca2+ and membranes, so they remain inactive.
explanation.whyCorrect: Warfarin inhibits vitamin K epoxide reductase, so reduced vitamin K cannot be regenerated. Without it, gamma-glutamyl carboxylase cannot carboxylate Glu residues to Gla, leaving factors II, VII, IX, X unable to bind Ca2+ and membranes, so they remain inactive.

questions.stem: This reaction sequence—beta-oxidation of phenylbutyrate to phenylacetate, activation to its CoA thioester, and conjugation with glutamine—represents the mechanism of which therapeutic strategy?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Nitrogen scavenging to lower plasma ammonia in urea cycle disorders

choices.label: B
choices.text: Suppressing intestinal urease-producing bacteria

choices.label: C
choices.text: Supplying arginine to restart a stalled urea cycle

choices.label: D
choices.text: Chelating ammonium ions for biliary excretion

choices.label: E
choices.text: Inhibiting hepatic glutaminase to reduce ammonia production

explanation.summary: Phenylacetate conjugates with glutamine to form phenylacetylglutamine, which is excreted renally and removes two nitrogen atoms per molecule, providing an alternate nitrogen-disposal route when the urea cycle is defective.
explanation.whyCorrect: Phenylacetate conjugates with glutamine to form phenylacetylglutamine, which is excreted renally and removes two nitrogen atoms per molecule, providing an alternate nitrogen-disposal route when the urea cycle is defective.

questions.stem: In this pathway benzoate is activated to benzoyl-CoA and then conjugated with an amino acid to form an excretable nitrogen-containing product. Which amino acid is consumed in this conjugation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Glutamine

choices.label: B
choices.text: Arginine

choices.label: C
choices.text: Glycine

choices.label: D
choices.text: Alanine

choices.label: E
choices.text: Aspartate

explanation.summary: Benzoate combines with glycine to form hippurate (benzoylglycine), which is excreted in urine; this consumes glycine's nitrogen and serves as an ammonia-scavenging therapy in urea cycle disorders.
explanation.whyCorrect: Benzoate combines with glycine to form hippurate (benzoylglycine), which is excreted in urine; this consumes glycine's nitrogen and serves as an ammonia-scavenging therapy in urea cycle disorders.
